macrophages.hccRole: TAMs are the most abundant immune cells in HCC, promoting progression through immunosuppression, angiogenesis, and matrix remodeling. Hypoxia polarizes them toward the M2 phenotype, enhancing immune evasion.
macrophages.prognosis: High TAM infiltration correlates with poor survival and sorafenib resistance
macrophages.therapy: CSF1R inhibitors (pexidartinib), CD47 blockade, repolarization strategies

smooth muscle cells.hccRole: VSMCs contribute to abnormal tumor vasculature, supporting poorly perfused vessels that perpetuate hypoxia. Hypoxia promotes their proliferation via PDGF signaling.
smooth muscle cells.prognosis: Increased VSMC coverage associates with anti-angiogenic therapy resistance
smooth muscle cells.therapy: Anti-angiogenic agents (sorafenib, lenvatinib) target VSMC-supported vasculature

fibroblasts.hccRole: CAFs originate from hepatic stellate cells and produce collagen, growth factors (HGF, FGF), and immunosuppressive cytokines. Hypoxia activates them via TGF-beta signaling.
fibroblasts.prognosis: Dense fibrotic stroma associates with poor drug penetration and therapy resistance

t-cells.hccRole: CD8+ T cells provide anti-tumor killing, but hypoxia upregulates PD-1, TIM-3, and LAG-3, driving exhaustion. Tregs accumulate under hypoxic conditions, suppressing immunity.
t-cells.prognosis: High CD8+ infiltration predicts better survival; high Treg/CD8 ratio predicts poor outcomes
t-cells.therapy: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (atezolizumab + bevacizumab), anti-LAG-3, anti-TIM-3

endothelial cells.hccRole: Liver sinusoidal endothelial cells lose fenestrations during HCC progression. Hypoxia drives VEGF-dependent angiogenesis, creating the hypervascular pattern characteristic of HCC.
endothelial cells.prognosis: Microvascular invasion is a strong predictor of recurrence after resection

dendritic cells.hccRole: DCs bridge innate and adaptive immunity. Hypoxia impairs their maturation and antigen presentation, promoting tolerogenic phenotypes that fail to prime anti-tumor T cells.
dendritic cells.prognosis: Low mature DC infiltration correlates with poor prognosis and reduced immunotherapy response
dendritic cells.therapy: DC vaccines, FLT3 ligand, STING agonists

granulocytes.hccRole: TANs promote HCC progression through NET formation, MMP9-mediated matrix degradation, and T cell suppression. Hypoxia prolongs neutrophil survival and promotes N2 polarization.
granulocytes.prognosis: High neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) predicts poor survival and therapy resistance
granulocytes.therapy: CXCR2 antagonists, PAD4 inhibitors, TGF-beta blockade

plasma cells.hccRole: Plasma cells in tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) can mediate anti-tumor ADCC. However, regulatory plasma cells produce immunosuppressive IL-10 and IL-35.
plasma cells.prognosis: TLS with plasma cells predicts better immunotherapy response; isolated IgA+ plasma cells may indicate immunosuppression
plasma cells.therapy: Strategies to induce TLS formation, CXCL13 agonism

hepatocytes.hccRole: Malignant hepatocytes are the tumor cells themselves. Hypoxia activates HIF-1alpha, driving the Warburg effect, EMT, stemness markers, and apoptosis resistance.
hepatocytes.prognosis: Hypoxic gene signatures in tumor hepatocytes correlate with aggressive phenotypes and macrovascular invasion
hepatocytes.therapy: HIF inhibitors (belzutifan), GPC3-targeted therapies, metabolic interventions

b-cells.hccRole: B cells in HCC can form tertiary lymphoid structures that support anti-tumor immunity, or produce regulatory cytokines that suppress it. Their role depends on location and differentiation state.
b-cells.prognosis: TLS-associated B cells predict favorable outcomes; regulatory B cells associate with progression
b-cells.therapy: B cell-directed strategies in combination with checkpoint inhibitors
